articles.title: ClickEle Transformer Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to selecting ClickEle power transformers based on topology, power rating, switching frequency, and application requirements.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: The most common mistake in transformer selection is undersizing the core for the power level. A transformer running too close to saturation will overheat and have poor regulation. I always recommend sizing the core with at least 20% margin above the actual power requirement. For example, for a 100W supply, use a transformer rated for 120-150W. Another key point: switching frequency matters. Higher frequencies allow smaller cores but increase losses. For industrial applications, 100kHz is a good compromise. ClickEle's CL-EF25 series is my go-to for 50-150W flyback designs - the EF core provides good power density with low profile.
faeInsights.logic: Transformer selection hierarchy: (1) Determine topology based on power and efficiency needs; (2) Calculate required power rating with margin; (3) Select core size based on power and frequency; (4) Choose core material for efficiency requirements; (5) Verify isolation and safety requirements; (6) Check thermal performance.
keyTakeaways: Select topology based on power level
Consider switching frequency for core sizing
bestPractices: Use current mode control to prevent saturation
Verify flux density <300mT at max Vin
Test thermal performance at max ambient
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience supporting diverse customer designs, this ClickEle Transformer Selection Guide article addresses the most common challenges engineers face in actual project development. The content derives from extensive real-case summaries and practical experience.
faeInsights.insightLogic: These professional recommendations come from in-depth analysis of hundreds of successful product deployment projects across industries, covering the full lifecycle from concept design to mass production.

articles.title: Power Inductor Selection Guide
articles.summary: Design guide for selecting power inductors including inductance calculation, saturation current, and core material selection.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: Inductor selection is about balancing inductance, current, and size. The key parameter most designers miss is saturation current. You need to calculate the peak current (DC + half the ripple) and ensure it's below the inductor's Isat rating. For a buck converter with 10A output and 2A ripple, peak is 11A - you need an inductor with Isat > 13A (20% margin). ClickEle's CL-PI series makes this easy - the part number indicates both inductance and current rating. CL-PI-100-4R7 is 4.7μH with 10A saturation. Another tip: DCR matters for efficiency. At 10A, 10mΩ DCR wastes 1W. The CL-PI-100 has 3.5mΩ DCR, saving 0.65W compared to a 10mΩ inductor.
faeInsights.logic: Inductor selection: (1) Calculate required inductance from ripple spec; (2) Calculate peak current (Idc + ΔI/2); (3) Select inductor with Isat > Ipeak × 1.2; (4) Check DCR for efficiency impact; (5) Verify SRF is >10× switching frequency; (6) Check temperature rise.
commonPitfalls: Using DC current instead of peak for Isat
SRF too close to switching frequency
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience supporting diverse customer designs, this Power Inductor Selection Guide article addresses the most common challenges engineers face in actual project development. The content derives from extensive real-case summaries and practical experience.
faeInsights.insightLogic: These professional recommendations come from in-depth analysis of hundreds of successful product deployment projects across industries, covering the full lifecycle from concept design to mass production.

articles.title: EMI Filter Design Guide
articles.summary: Best practices for designing EMI filters including common mode choke selection, differential filtering, and layout guidelines.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: EMI filter design is often the difference between passing and failing EMC testing. The key component is the common mode choke - and nanocrystalline cores make a huge difference. A 10mH nanocrystalline choke provides the same high-frequency impedance as a 50mH ferrite choke, but in a much smaller package. ClickEle's CL-CMC series uses nanocrystalline material, giving you 5-10x better performance than ferrite alternatives. For layout, the #1 rule is place the CMC right at the input connector. Every millimeter of trace between connector and CMC reduces effectiveness. Also, use a two-stage filter for >30dB attenuation - one CMC near input, another near the converter, with capacitors between.
faeInsights.logic: EMI filter design: (1) Measure baseline noise without filter; (2) Calculate required attenuation vs limits; (3) Select CMC with >1kΩ impedance at fsw; (4) Add differential mode filtering if needed; (5) Place CMC at input connector; (6) Use two stages for high attenuation.
keyTakeaways: Use nanocrystalline CMC for best performance
commonPitfalls: Using ferrite CMC for high-frequency noise
bestPractices: Test with actual load, not resistor
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience supporting diverse customer designs, this EMI Filter Design Guide article addresses the most common challenges engineers face in actual project development. The content derives from extensive real-case summaries and practical experience.

articles.title: Magnetics Troubleshooting Guide
articles.summary: Common magnetic component issues and diagnostic procedures including transformer saturation, inductor heating, and EMI failures.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: When troubleshooting magnetics issues, always start with measurements. For transformer saturation, use a current probe on the primary - you'll see the current ramp up non-linearly if saturating. The slope should be linear for proper operation. For overheating inductors, check both DC and AC current - sometimes the ripple current is higher than expected, causing excess core loss. An infrared camera is invaluable for finding hot spots. For EMI issues, the spectrum analyzer is your best friend. Look for the fundamental switching frequency and harmonics. Common mode noise above 1MHz is usually layout-related - check for coupling between primary and secondary. One often-overlooked cause: insufficient Y capacitance. For a 100W supply, you typically need 2-4nF of Y cap to ground.
faeInsights.logic: Troubleshooting approach: (1) Measure primary current waveform for saturation; (2) Check inductor temperature and current; (3) Analyze EMI spectrum for noise sources; (4) Verify layout against best practices; (5) Test components individually; (6) Review design calculations.
keyTakeaways: Use current probe to check for saturation
Measure both DC and AC inductor current
Analyze EMI spectrum for problem frequencies
commonPitfalls: Ignoring AC ripple in inductor heating
bestPractices: Use current mode control to prevent saturation
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience supporting diverse customer designs, this Magnetics Troubleshooting Guide article addresses the most common challenges engineers face in actual project development. The content derives from extensive real-case summaries and practical experience.
